| 1   | TC01 — Register a new user (happy path)                                                            | `public`, `features_m2`    | `POST registerPath()`                                                                                                                                                                                                 | The S1-F1 register endpoint must persist a brand-new user account to PostgreSQL and respond with HTTP 2xx plus a body containing the newly-assigned numeric `id`. This maps to the spec requirement that `POST /api/auth/register` with valid fields (name, email, password, phone) writes a row to the `users` table and echoes that row's id to the caller. A strict 2xx assertion is intentional: a fresh nonce-based email with a valid payload must succeed unconditionally — any non-2xx is a bug in the controller, the JSR-303 validators, the BCrypt encoder, or the JPA save call. The numeric-id check catches handlers that return only `{"message":"ok"}` without echoing the persisted row's identifier, leaving clients with no handle to reference the new user.                                                                                                                                                                                    | 1) Build a JSON register body with a `nonce()`-suffixed email (e.g. `tc01_<nonce>@grader.testgen.io`), a fixed name (`"TC01 User"`), a fresh phone, and a strong password (`TestPwd!2026`) — the nonce prevents collision with auto-seeded `_preseed_*` users or earlier runs of this test class.<br>2) POST `registerPath()` (resolved through the manifest, typically `/api/auth/register`) with `Content-Type: application/json` and the body above.<br>3) Read the HTTP status code and assert 2xx via `assert2xx`.<br>4) Parse the response body as JSON.<br>5) Inspect the `id` field for non-null and verify its JSON node type is numeric.                                       | • Status 200..299<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Validation rejects a perfectly-valid payload (`@NotBlank`/`@Email` mis-configured), or the persistence path throws (BCrypt encoder not wired, schema column missing, transaction not committing) and the exception leaks as 5xx instead of being handled.<br>• Body has non-null `id` field<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Controller returns `{"message":"ok"}` or an empty body instead of echoing the saved row — clients then have no identifier to reference the new user in subsequent calls (login, profile, etc.).<br>• `id` is numeric<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** `id` is serialised as a string (`"id":"42"`) — breaks `Long` deserialization on Jackson clients and signals a sloppy DTO with a `String`-typed id field.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
| 2   | TC02 — Login with valid credentials (happy path)                                                   | `public`, `features_m2`    | `POST registerPath()` (setup), `POST loginPath()`                                                                                                                                                                     | The S1-F2 login endpoint must verify the supplied credentials against the bcrypt hash that register persisted, and on a match mint a signed JWT and return it under the `token` field. The 3-segment dot-split check is the lightest possible structural test that the returned value is actually a JWT (header.payload.signature) rather than a placeholder string, an opaque session id, or a hand-rolled `username:role` token. Strict 2xx is required because the credentials match the just-registered user — anything else is a real bug. This catches the common bcrypt-roundtrip mismatch (register stores in one format, login compares in another), missing token-issuance code, and naive implementations that return an unsigned/two-segment value or a UUID.                                                                                                                                                                                           | 1) Build a register body identical in shape to TC01 with a unique nonce-based email and a known plaintext password (`TestPwd!2026`).<br>2) POST `registerPath()` and assert 2xx as a precondition — login can't succeed if register failed.<br>3) Build a login body `{"email":"<same>","password":"TestPwd!2026"}`.<br>4) POST `loginPath()` (typically `/api/auth/login`) with that body.<br>5) Assert 2xx, parse the response as JSON, assert `token` is present and non-blank, then split by `.` and verify exactly 3 segments.                                                                                                                                                      | • Login status 200..299<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Bcrypt hash format mismatch between register (`encode`) and login (`matches`) so legitimate credentials are rejected as 401, or the login endpoint is missing entirely (404).<br>• Body has non-blank `token` field<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Controller returns 2xx but forgets to include the token (`{"message":"login ok"}`) — clients then cannot make any authenticated calls.<br>• Token splits into exactly 3 dot-separated segments (`a.b.c` JWT shape)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Token is a plaintext UUID, an opaque session cookie, or a hand-rolled `username:role` string instead of a real signed JWT — meaning no signature, no claims, no expiry, and no actual security.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
| 3   | TC03 — Read own user profile with valid JWT (happy path)                                           | `public`, `updated_crud`   | `POST registerPath()` (setup), `POST loginPath()` (setup), `GET crudReadPath("User")`                                                                                                                                 | The JWT minted by login is accepted by the security filter chain, the User CRUD GET-by-id endpoint passes the owner authorization check, and the user's own profile is returned as a JSON object. This is the end-to-end "I just registered, can I see myself" path — the simplest possible owner-read scenario. A strict 2xx is required (no `2xx OR 404` escape) because the test just registered this exact user, so a 404 here means the controller's GET-by-id path is fundamentally broken or the JWT chain rejected our own valid token. The JSON-object body check rules out controllers that return `200 OK` with an array, a string, or an empty body. Together this catches JWT filter bugs (signature verification failing), broken claim extraction (`uid` missing from token), and CRUD-authorization-chain bugs where the owner check incorrectly compares email vs id.                                                                              | 1) Register a fresh nonce-based user via `POST registerPath()` and assert 2xx.<br>2) Decode the registration response to capture the user's numeric `id` — or pull the `uid` claim out of the JWT via `uidFromJwt`.<br>3) POST `loginPath()` with the same email + password and capture the `token`.<br>4) GET `crudReadPathFor("User", id)` (typically `/api/users/<id>`) with `Authorization: Bearer <token>`.<br>5) Assert strict 2xx (no 404 escape) and parse the body as a JSON object.                                                                                                                                                                                            | • GET status 200..299 (strict, not the `2xx OR 404` escape)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Owner-read endpoint is missing entirely (404), or the security filter chain rejects a valid token (401) due to a mis-wired `JwtAuthenticationFilter` or a missing `OncePerRequestFilter` registration.<br>• Response body parses as a JSON object<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Controller returns an empty body, a wrapped envelope, or an array instead of the user resource — clients can't extract profile fields.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| 4   | TC04 — Register with duplicate email returns 4xx (negative path)                                   | `public`, `features_m2`    | `POST registerPath()` (called twice)                                                                                                                                                                                  | The register endpoint must enforce email uniqueness at the persistence layer and translate the conflict into a clean 4xx client error. Per S1-F1, the `User.email` column carries a unique constraint and the controller is expected to catch the resulting `DataIntegrityViolationException` (or pre-check via the repository) and return 400/409/422. The 4xx assertion is bracketed strictly: NOT 2xx (which would mean two accounts with the same email exist), NOT 5xx (uncaught DB exception leaking — the spec requires graceful conflict handling), and NOT 401/403 (the body itself is well-formed and authorized — those codes signal the controller is mis-classifying the error). This catches a missing `@Column(unique=true)` on `User.email`, swallowed exceptions, silent 2xx accepts that create duplicate rows, and ham-fisted error-handling that returns 500 on any DB error.                                                                   | 1) Build a register body with a `nonce()`-suffixed email + valid password + phone.<br>2) POST `registerPath()` and assert 2xx — this is the precondition that establishes the email is now in use.<br>3) Build a second register body with the same email but a different name and phone (uniqueness is on email alone, not the whole row).<br>4) POST `registerPath()` again with the duplicate-email body.<br>5) Read the response status and assert it is in 400..499, not 401/403, and not 5xx.                                                                                                                                                                                      | • First register status 200..299<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Setup precondition broken — the rest of the test is meaningless if the first register failed.<br>• Second register status 400..499 (strict — must be a client error)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Controller returns 2xx and creates a second row (no unique constraint, or constraint not enforced because Hibernate validation is off), or returns 5xx because the integrity violation was not caught.<br>• NOT 5xx (no server crash on the duplicate)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** `DataIntegrityViolationException` propagates uncaught — clients see a stack trace dump and have no actionable error message.<br>• NOT 2xx (uniqueness must be enforced)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Critical data-integrity bug: two users with the same email exist, breaking login (which user does the email belong to?) and breaking cross-service joins.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| 5   | TC05 — Login with wrong password returns 401 (negative path)                                       | `public`, `features_m2`    | `POST registerPath()` (setup), `POST loginPath()`                                                                                                                                                                     | Login authenticates the supplied password against the stored bcrypt hash and rejects mismatches with strictly `401 Unauthorized`. Per S1-F2 the spec requires a uniform 401 for any auth failure on existing accounts (wrong password), and this test exercises the wrong-password leg. The strictness is deliberate: 404 here would leak whether the account exists (OWASP user-enumeration anti-pattern) and 403 is structurally wrong because login is the act of obtaining permissions, not exercising them. This catches login implementations that skip the password check and silently return a token (catastrophic security bug), NPE on missing `passwordEncoder.matches`, bcrypt format mismatches that throw 5xx, and 404-vs-401 misclassification.                                                                                                                                                                                                      | 1) Register a fresh nonce-based user with `password=TestPwd!2026` and assert 2xx — this proves the account exists with the correct hash.<br>2) Build a login body with the same email but a clearly different password (`WrongPwd!2026` — different prefix and length so bcrypt cannot accidentally match).<br>3) POST `loginPath()` with the wrong-password body.<br>4) Read the status code and assert strictly 401, with explicit guards against 2xx, 5xx, 404, and 403.                                                                                                                                                                                                              | • Register status 200..299 (precondition)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Setup failure — invalidates the rest of the test.<br>• Login status strictly 401<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Anything other than 401 is a misclassification of authentication failure.<br>• NOT 2xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Login skipped the password check and minted a token anyway — critical security bug (anyone with any password can impersonate any user).<br>• NOT 5xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Bcrypt mismatch threw an unhandled exception (e.g., `IllegalArgumentException` from a malformed hash) that leaked instead of being caught and translated to 401.<br>• NOT 404<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** OWASP user-enumeration leak — returning 404 vs 401 distinguishes "user exists but wrong password" from "no such user", letting attackers enumerate valid emails.<br>• NOT 403<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Controller treats login as a permissioned action (mapping any auth failure to 403 generically) instead of distinguishing "not authenticated" (401) from "authenticated but lacking permission" (403).             |
| 6   | TC06 — Authentication happy path (valid admin JWT accepted on a non-User CRUD list)                | `public`, `authentication` | `adminToken()` (setup; logs in pre-seeded admin OR registers + promotes one), `GET crudCollectionPath(firstTopLevelNonUserEntity())` with Bearer token                                                                | The JWT auth pipeline must accept a valid admin token on a controller other than UserController, proving the security filter chain is registered globally (`OncePerRequestFilter` applied to every dispatcher type) rather than scoped to `/api/users/**` only. An ADMIN token is used so that role-protected list endpoints (which would 403 a regular ATTENDEE) cannot mask an auth-filter bug — the test isolates filter wiring from role enforcement. For Event Ticketing, the target entity resolved by `firstTopLevelNonUserEntity()` is `Event` (path `/api/events`), the first non-User entity in the manifest. This is the A/B partner of TC07: same endpoint, only the Authorization header differs — together they prove the protection is real and not decorative.                                                                                                                                                                                      | 1) Call `adminToken()` to obtain a real admin Bearer JWT.<br>2) Resolve the first top-level non-User entity from the manifest via `firstTopLevelNonUserEntity()` — for Event Ticketing this returns `"Event"`.<br>3) Compute its CRUD list path via `crudCollectionPath("Event")` (typically `/api/events`).<br>4) GET that path with header `Authorization: Bearer <token>`.<br>5) Assert strict 2xx via `assert2xx`.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   | • GET status 200..299 (strict — proves auth filter accepted the admin token on the Event CRUD list)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Auth filter is registered only for `/api/users/**` (e.g., via `requestMatchers("/api/users/**")` in HttpSecurity), leaving `/api/events/**` either wide-open (paired with TC07's failure) or rejecting valid admin tokens. Also catches admins not getting the role they claim (token issued without role claim, so role-aware controllers 403 a real admin).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| 7   | TC07 — Missing Authorization header on a non-User CRUD list returns 401 (negative path)            | `public`, `authentication` | `GET crudCollectionPath(firstTopLevelNonUserEntity())` with NO Authorization header                                                                                                                                   | The JWT auth filter must block anonymous requests across all controllers, not just UserController, returning strictly 401. This is the A/B partner of TC06: same endpoint (`/api/events` for Event Ticketing), only the Authorization header is removed. Together TC06+TC07 prove the protection is global and real — passing TC06 alone could just mean the endpoint is `permitAll`. The strict 401 (NOT 404, NOT 403, NOT 5xx) is enforced because each non-401 code masks a distinct bug class: 404 means the filter passed the request through to a controller that then returned empty data (auth never blocked), 403 means the controller is mis-classifying anonymous as "authenticated but unauthorized", and 5xx means the filter chain threw rather than cleanly rejecting. This catches missing `OncePerRequestFilter` registration, accidental `.permitAll()` on the target controller, and controllers exposed outside the security pipeline entirely. | 1) Resolve the first top-level non-User entity from the manifest via `firstTopLevelNonUserEntity()` — for Event Ticketing this returns `"Event"`, same target as TC06.<br>2) Compute the CRUD list path via `crudCollectionPath("Event")` (typically `/api/events`).<br>3) GET that path with `httpGet` (the no-auth variant — explicitly does not send any Authorization header).<br>4) Assert the status code is strictly 401.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         | • GET status strictly 401<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Anything other than 401 means the auth pipeline is misconfigured for the Event controller.<br>• NOT 2xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Endpoint is wide-open — critical security bug exposing event data to anonymous callers.<br>• NOT 5xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Filter chain crashed (e.g., NPE on missing token) instead of cleanly rejecting — a properly-engineered filter must handle the absent-header case as the most common path.<br>• NOT 404<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** The endpoint was reachable without auth and just returned empty results — wrong; auth must block FIRST, before any controller logic decides "no events to return".<br>• NOT 403<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** No credentials were sent; 403 means "authenticated but lacking permission", which is structurally inappropriate for this case. The student is conflating the two failure modes.                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| 8   | TC08 — Tampered JWT signature is rejected with 401 (negative path)                                 | `public`, `authentication` | `adminToken()` (setup), `GET crudCollectionPath(firstTopLevelNonUserEntity())` with **tampered** Bearer token                                                                                                         | The auth filter must perform real cryptographic verification of the JWT signature, not merely decode the structure. This is the third and most critical leg of the auth-filter triad — TC06 (valid → 2xx), TC07 (no token → 401), TC08 (forged token → 401) — and together they prove the filter does real signature checking. The catastrophic security bug it targets is when a student extracts claims (`uid`, `role`) directly from the base64-decoded payload without validating that the signature matches: an attacker can then forge any token by editing the payload and re-base64'ing, with the original or any signature, and gain unauthorized access. Strict 401 because a tampered signature is exactly the case auth must reject — 403 would be a misclassification, 404 means the filter passed the bad token through to the controller.                                                                                                            | 1) `adminToken()` — get a real, fully-valid admin JWT.<br>2) `tamperSignature(token)` — keep the original header and payload intact, but replace the third (signature) segment with the base64-url of `"tampered-signature-does-not-verify"`. The result still parses as a JWT (3 dot-separated segments, valid base64) but the signature is mathematically wrong.<br>3) Resolve `firstTopLevelNonUserEntity()` and compute `crudCollectionPath("Event")` (same endpoint family as TC06/TC07).<br>4) GET that path with `Authorization: Bearer <tampered-token>`.<br>5) Assert strictly 401.                                                                                             | • GET status strictly 401<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Filter is not verifying signatures correctly. Anything else means the signature check is broken or absent.<br>• NOT 2xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Critical security bug — the signature is not being verified. Code like `Jwts.parser().setSigningKey(key).parseClaimsJws(token).getBody()` may have been replaced with `Jwts.parser().parseClaimsJwt(token)` (no signature check) or claims read directly from the base64 payload. Anyone can forge tokens.<br>• NOT 5xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** `SignatureException`/`MalformedJwtException` is leaking unhandled instead of being caught and translated to 401.<br>• NOT 404<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Filter passed the request through to the controller — signature mismatch must be caught FIRST in the security chain, not deferred to a 404 from the data layer.<br>• NOT 403<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Forged credentials are "not authenticated" → 401, not "authenticated but lacking permission" → 403. Returning 403 here means the student is mis-classifying signature failures as permission errors. |
| 9   | TC09 — Login with non-existent email returns 404 (negative path)                                   | `public`, `features_m2`    | `POST loginPath()` (with email never registered)                                                                                                                                                                      | Login distinguishes "user not found" (404) from "user found but wrong password" (401, covered by TC05) per the spec, which explicitly maps the two failure modes to different status codes. The test uses a nonce-suffixed email guaranteed never to exist — so the only correct response is 404. Strict 404 (not 401, not 2xx, not 5xx) because the spec is explicit; the two auth-failure codes are intentionally split here (404 vs 401) unlike OWASP's recommendation to unify them. This catches login implementations that throw NPE on `Optional.get()` for a missing user, return 401 generically (defeating the spec's distinction), or return 2xx with a token for accounts that don't exist (catastrophic).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              | 1) Generate a nonce-suffixed email that has NEVER been registered (e.g., `tc09_<nonce>@grader.testgen.io`).<br>2) Build a login body of `{"email":"<nonce-email>","password":"AnythingNonEmpty"}`.<br>3) POST `loginPath()` with that body.<br>4) Read the response status code and assert strictly 404.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 | • Status strictly 404<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Anything else is a misclassification of the not-found condition.<br>• NOT 2xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** A token was issued for a user that does not exist — catastrophic; suggests login bypassed the user lookup or fell through to a default `findById`.<br>• NOT 5xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** `Optional.get()` on an empty `Optional` threw NoSuchElementException, or the controller didn't anticipate the missing-user case. Login must always handle the missing-user path gracefully.<br>• NOT 403<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Controller mapped "user not found" to a permission failure — structurally wrong; the user simply doesn't exist, no permission decision is involved.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
| 10  | TC10 — Empty Bearer token returns 401 (negative path)                                              | `public`, `authentication` | `GET crudCollectionPath(firstTopLevelNonUserEntity())` with `Authorization: Bearer ` (no token after Bearer prefix)                                                                                                   | The auth filter must reject malformed Authorization headers where the value after the `Bearer ` scheme is empty, returning strictly 401. This is a sibling negative test to TC08 (forged signature) and TC11 (wrong scheme), each targeting a different malformed-header class. Strict 401 is required — 5xx here means the JWT parser threw NPE on an empty string instead of being defensively coded. This catches (a) `parseToken("")` NPE because `String.split(".")` on an empty string returns a length-1 array, (b) `if (token != null)` checks that pass empty strings through, and (c) accidentally treating empty Bearer as anonymous.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    | 1) Resolve `firstTopLevelNonUserEntity()` and compute `crudCollectionPath("Event")` (i.e., `/api/events`).<br>2) Call `httpGetWithRawAuth(path, "Bearer ")` — the helper sets the header literally as `Authorization: Bearer ` (trailing space, empty token portion), bypassing the auto-trim that some HTTP libraries do.<br>3) Read the response status and assert strictly 401.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | • Status strictly 401<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Empty Bearer accepted (2xx) or parser-NPE leaked (5xx).<br>• NOT 2xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Filter treats `"Bearer "` as a successful credential because it sees the prefix is correct and skips the value-non-empty check.<br>• NOT 5xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** JWT parser threw `IllegalArgumentException` or NPE on the empty value because the filter didn't validate non-empty before parsing — must short-circuit defensively.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
| 11  | TC11 — Non-Bearer scheme (Basic) returns 401 (negative path)                                       | `public`, `authentication` | `GET crudCollectionPath(firstTopLevelNonUserEntity())` with `Authorization: Basic dXNlcjpwYXNz`                                                                                                                       | The auth filter must reject Authorization headers that use any scheme other than `Bearer`, returning strictly 401. The spec mandates JWT-Bearer auth as the only accepted scheme, and Basic auth is the most realistic mistaken alternative. This catches lenient parsers that strip the leading word and try to decode whatever's left as a JWT — a `"Basic dXNlcjpwYXNz"` header would then be interpreted as a token of `dXNlcjpwYXNz`, causing parse failure (5xx) or accidental rejection (401 for the wrong reason). The filter must explicitly check the scheme is `Bearer` before reading the credential.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   | 1) Resolve `firstTopLevelNonUserEntity()` and compute `crudCollectionPath("Event")` (i.e., `/api/events`).<br>2) Call `httpGetWithRawAuth(path, "Basic dXNlcjpwYXNz")` — the value `dXNlcjpwYXNz` is base64 of `"user:pass"`, the canonical Basic-auth example.<br>3) Read the response status and assert strictly 401.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | • Status strictly 401<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Filter does not validate the scheme; non-Bearer schemes must be rejected.<br>• NOT 2xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Filter accepted Basic auth — implies an alternate auth path was wired in by accident or the scheme check is missing entirely.<br>• NOT 5xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Filter blindly substring-strips `"Bearer "` from the header value (`header.substring(7)`) without first verifying the prefix; on `"Basic ..."` this reads garbage into the JWT parser which then throws. The filter must classify cleanly.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| 12  | TC12 — Garbage non-JWT token returns 401 (negative path)                                           | `public`, `authentication` | `GET crudCollectionPath(firstTopLevelNonUserEntity())` with `Authorization: Bearer not_a_valid_jwt`                                                                                                                   | The auth filter must reject values that are syntactically invalid as JWTs (no dots, no base64 segments) with strictly 401. Together with TC10 (empty), TC11 (wrong scheme), TC08 (tampered signature), this completes the malformed-input matrix. The strict 5xx prohibition is the key catch: parsers that throw `MalformedJwtException` or `IllegalArgumentException` on garbage input must have those exceptions caught at the filter level and translated to 401, not propagated as 500. This catches parsers wired with `parseClaimsJws` directly inside the controller (no exception handling) and "if 3 dot-segments, decode without verifying" anti-patterns that fall apart on inputs that don't even have 3 segments.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     | 1) Resolve `firstTopLevelNonUserEntity()` and compute `crudCollectionPath("Event")` (i.e., `/api/events`).<br>2) Call `httpGetWithRawAuth(path, "Bearer not_a_valid_jwt")` — a value with no dots and no valid base64 segments, guaranteed to fail every JWT structure check.<br>3) Read the response status and assert strictly 401.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    | • Status strictly 401<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Filter does not handle malformed JWT inputs gracefully.<br>• NOT 2xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Garbage was accepted — implies the filter never tried to parse the value, a dangerous footgun where any random string passes auth.<br>• NOT 5xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Parser exception (e.g., `MalformedJwtException`) propagated to the response — the filter must catch parse exceptions and translate to 401, not crash the request.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| 13  | TC13 — Forged role-claim token (payload modified post-signing) is rejected (negative path)         | `public`, `authentication` | `POST registerPath()` (setup), `POST loginPath()` (setup), `GET crudCollectionPath(firstTopLevelNonUserEntity())` with payload-tampered Bearer token                                                                  | The signature must cover the payload contents, not just the JWT structure. The test takes a legitimately-issued ATTENDEE token, modifies the payload's `role` claim from `ATTENDEE` to `ADMIN` (or injects the claim if absent), and reattaches the original signature unchanged. A correctly-implemented filter computes the signature over the (tampered) payload at verification time and finds it does not match the supplied (original) signature, so rejects with 401. The assertion is `NOT 2xx` rather than strict 401 because two correct implementations exist: filter-level rejection (401 from signature check) or server-side role re-validation against the DB (which would produce 403). Both are acceptable; 2xx means the signature was never verified, which is the exact catastrophic bug being targeted — student code that reads `role` from the base64-decoded payload without re-running the HMAC.                                           | 1) Register a fresh ATTENDEE user via `POST registerPath()` and assert 2xx as a precondition.<br>2) Login via `POST loginPath()` and capture the issued JWT.<br>3) Decode the payload segment (`token.split(".")[1]`) from base64-url; if the payload contains `"role":"ATTENDEE"`, replace it with `"role":"ADMIN"`; otherwise inject `"role":"ADMIN"`. Re-encode the modified payload to base64-url and reassemble as `header + "." + tamperedPayload + "." + originalSignature`.<br>4) Resolve `firstTopLevelNonUserEntity()` and compute `crudCollectionPath("Event")`.<br>5) GET that path with `Authorization: Bearer <forged-token>`.<br>6) Assert status is NOT 2xx and NOT 5xx. | • Status NOT 2xx (forged token must not grant access)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** The most catastrophic JWT bug possible — privilege escalation by editing the JSON payload. Student code likely reads role directly from base64-decoded payload (`role = decode(token.split(".")[1]).get("role")`) without re-verifying the HMAC. Fixing requires using `Jwts.parser().setSigningKey(key).parseClaimsJws(token).getBody().get("role")` so the parse fails on signature mismatch.<br>• NOT 5xx (filter must reject cleanly)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** `SignatureException` from the JWT library leaked unhandled to the response — should be caught and translated to 401.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| 14  | TC14 — Register with missing required field returns 4xx (negative path)                            | `public`, `features_m2`    | `POST registerPath()` (with body missing email)                                                                                                                                                                       | The register endpoint must validate required fields up-front and return 4xx for any incomplete payload. Per the S1-F1 spec, `email` is required and must be unique. The test omits email entirely (not blank, not null in JSON, but missing as a key) and expects the controller to short-circuit with 400 before ever reaching the persistence layer. Strict 4xx (with explicit NOT 5xx) is the contract: invalid input is a client error, not a server error. This catches controllers that skip `@Valid`/`@Validated` annotations on the request DTO, allowing the missing field to fall through into the service layer where it triggers a `NullPointerException` or a `NOT NULL` DB constraint violation that leaks as 5xx.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    | 1) Build a register body with `name` + `password` + `phone` but NO `email` field at all (omitted entirely from the JSON object — not `"email":""` and not `"email":null`).<br>2) POST `registerPath()` with `Content-Type: application/json` and that body.<br>3) Read the response status and assert it is in 400..499 and not 5xx.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     | • Status 400..499 (strict 4xx — must be a client validation error)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Controller didn't validate the input before passing to the service.<br>• NOT 2xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** A user row was created with `email=NULL` — would either hit a NOT NULL constraint (if the schema is correct) or worse, persist a meaningless row that breaks login forever.<br>• NOT 5xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** `@Valid` is missing on the controller's `@RequestBody` parameter, so `email == null` propagates into the service which NPEs or hits a DB constraint. The validation must run BEFORE the service layer via Bean Validation.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| 15  | TC15 — Register with role=ADMIN in body must NOT result in an ADMIN account (privilege-escalation) | `public`, `features_m2`    | `POST registerPath()` (with `role:"ADMIN"` in body), JDBC role lookup via `fetchUserRole(email)`                                                                                                                      | This is one of the most important security tests in the suite: a critical mass-assignment / privilege-escalation check. The test registers a user with `"role":"ADMIN"` injected into the request body and verifies that the resulting account does NOT end up with ADMIN role in the database. The controller is allowed to handle the extra field in three ways — silently ignore (best), reject with 400, or accept as ATTENDEE — but the key is that the role MUST NOT be honored from request input. The DB-level check (via `fetchUserRole`) is critical because it does not depend on the response body being correct; it inspects the persisted state directly. This catches controllers that blindly map JSON to entity (`new User(req)`, `BeanUtils.copyProperties`, or `@RequestBody User user`) with no DTO/allow-list, letting any client self-promote to ADMIN by adding one field.                                                                   | 1) Build a register body with the standard fields (name, email, password, phone) PLUS `"role":"ADMIN"`.<br>2) POST `registerPath()` and assert NOT 5xx — registration with an extra field should not crash; the field should just be ignored or stripped.<br>3) Use `fetchUserRole(email)` (which reads `auth.userTable`/`roleColumn`/`emailColumn` from the manifest and runs a JDBC SELECT) to look up the persisted role for the new user.<br>4) Assert the role is NOT `"ADMIN"`.<br>5) Optionally soft-assert the role equals the theme default (`"ATTENDEE"` for Event Ticketing).                                                                                                 | • Register status NOT 5xx (registration with extra field shouldn't crash)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Controller is too strict and rejects unknown fields with 5xx when the spec says to ignore them, or Bean Validation misconfigured to throw on extra properties.<br>• Resulting role in DB NOT EQUAL `"ADMIN"`<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** The catastrophic mass-assignment vulnerability — student used `@RequestBody User user; userRepository.save(user);` which honors every field including role. Anyone can create an admin account by adding two characters to a registration JSON. Fix: use a `RegisterRequest` DTO with only the safe fields, or strip the role field server-side.<br>• Optional soft check: role equals `"ATTENDEE"` (theme default)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Default role is wrong (e.g., null, ADMIN, or STAFF instead of ATTENDEE) — indicates the bootstrap path is broken for this theme.                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |
| 16  | TC16 — Login with empty password returns 4xx (negative path)                                       | `public`, `features_m2`    | `POST registerPath()` (setup), `POST loginPath()` (with `password:""`)                                                                                                                                                | Login must validate that the password field is non-empty before invoking bcrypt comparison. A real registered user is created with a valid password, then the test attempts to log in with the same email but `password:""`. The expected response is 400 (bad request — validation failure) or 401 (auth failure), both acceptable since the spec doesn't pin a specific code for this edge case — but NOT 2xx and NOT 5xx. This catches a subtle but dangerous bug: some bcrypt implementations and some hand-rolled `passwordEncoder.matches` shims treat empty input as a no-op match (returning true) or NPE on the empty string. Either failure mode is catastrophic — empty-string acceptance means anyone can log in as any user with no password.                                                                                                                                                                                                          | 1) Register a fresh nonce-based user with `password=TestPwd!2026` and assert 2xx.<br>2) Build a login body `{"email":"<same>","password":""}` — explicitly empty string, not null and not missing.<br>3) POST `loginPath()` with that body.<br>4) Read the status and assert NOT 2xx and NOT 5xx; 400 or 401 are both acceptable.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        | • Status NOT 2xx (empty password must not yield a token)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Empty-string password matched the stored hash — catastrophic auth bypass. Caused by `passwordEncoder.matches("", hash)` returning true in a buggy implementation, or by the controller short-circuiting bcrypt entirely when the input is empty.<br>• NOT 5xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Empty-string input threw NPE in the password handling (e.g., `password.toCharArray()` on null) — must be caught and translated to 400/401.<br>• Acceptable: 400 (bad request — empty password rejected by validation) or 401 (auth failure — password mismatch)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Each is a clean handling path; spec is intentionally lenient here.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
| 17  | TC17 — Cross-user IDOR: User A cannot READ User B's profile (negative path)                        | `public`, `authorization`  | `POST registerPath()` × 2 (setup A + B), `POST loginPath()` (setup A), `GET crudReadPathFor("User", B.id)` with A's token                                                                                             | The User CRUD GET-by-id endpoint must enforce ownership: an ATTENDEE can only read their own profile, never another user's. This tests the classic IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) vulnerability where the `{id}` path variable is trusted as the lookup key without verifying it matches the authenticated principal's id. The spec accepts either 403 (deny — server admits the resource exists but blocks access) or 404 (privacy-by-obscurity — server pretends the resource doesn't exist for unauthorized callers); both are spec-conformant. NOT 5xx because a missing ownership check should not crash, and NOT 2xx because returning B's data to A is a critical privacy breach. This catches the most common IDOR bug pattern: `userRepository.findById(pathId)` with no comparison against `authenticatedUser.id`.                                                                                                                               | 1) Register attendee A via `POST registerPath()` and assert 2xx.<br>2) Register attendee B via `POST registerPath()` and capture B's `id` from the response.<br>3) Login as A via `POST loginPath()` and capture A's token.<br>4) GET `crudReadPathFor("User", B.id)` with header `Authorization: Bearer <A's token>`.<br>5) Read the status and assert NOT 2xx, NOT 5xx, and either 403 or 404.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         | • Status NOT 2xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** A read B's profile — critical privacy breach. Likely cause: controller does `findById(pathId)` and returns the result with no ownership check.<br>• Status 403 (deny) OR 404 (privacy-by-obscurity)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Each is a valid handling style; the test is lenient because the spec doesn't pin one.<br>• NOT 5xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Ownership check threw an exception (e.g., NPE because `SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication()` returned an unexpected principal type) — must be defensively coded.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| 18  | TC18 — Cross-user IDOR: User A cannot UPDATE User B's profile (negative path)                      | `public`, `authorization`  | `POST registerPath()` × 2 (setup A + B), `POST loginPath()` (setup A), `PUT crudReadPathFor("User", B.id)` with A's token + tampered name                                                                             | Cross-user write protection extends the IDOR check to PUT requests. The PUT body includes all of B's originally-registered fields plus a hijacked name (mitigation pattern: include all fields so a sloppy controller can't fail with a "missing field" 400 and accidentally pass the test). The DEFENSIVE JDBC check is the critical addition over TC17: even if the response is 403, the test then queries the database directly to confirm B's name was actually NOT modified. This catches a sneaky bug class where the controller returns 403 but commits the update before the security check runs (out-of-order business logic), or where the security check throws but the transaction is already committed.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                | 1) Register A and B; capture B's id from the response.<br>2) Login as A and capture A's token.<br>3) Build a PUT body containing `"name":"TC18 HIJACK"` plus B's original email/password/phone (so the body shape is valid).<br>4) PUT `crudReadPathFor("User", B.id)` with `Authorization: Bearer <A's token>` and the body above.<br>5) Read the status and assert NOT 2xx and NOT 5xx (must be 403 or 404).<br>6) JDBC: `SELECT name FROM <tableName("User")> WHERE id = B.id` and assert it equals `"TC18 B Original"` (unchanged).                                                                                                                                                  | • Status NOT 2xx (must be 403 or 404)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Cross-user write succeeded — A modified B's profile.<br>• NOT 5xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Authorization check threw uncaught.<br>• JDBC: B's name unchanged from `"TC18 B Original"`<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Response said 403 but the DB row was actually modified — the security check happens AFTER the update, either due to ordering bug or because the transaction commits before the check fails. The fix is to enforce ownership BEFORE writing, ideally with a `@PreAuthorize` annotation that prevents the method body from running.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
| 19  | TC19 — Cross-user IDOR: User A cannot DELETE User B (negative path)                                | `public`, `authorization`  | `POST registerPath()` × 2 (setup A + B), `POST loginPath()` (setup A), `DELETE crudReadPathFor("User", B.id)` with A's token                                                                                          | Cross-user delete protection. The most consequential of the IDOR-triad (TC17 read, TC18 update, TC19 delete) because deletion is irreversible — a successful cross-user delete cannot be recovered without a backup. Same pattern as TC18: response status must NOT be 2xx (403 or 404 acceptable), AND the JDBC count of B's row must still be 1 to defensively confirm the row was not actually deleted before the 403 was returned. This catches the same out-of-order security bug pattern as TC18 plus the broader class of `userRepository.deleteById(pathId)` calls that don't verify ownership at all.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      | 1) Register A and B; capture B's id.<br>2) Login as A and capture A's token.<br>3) DELETE `crudReadPathFor("User", B.id)` with `Authorization: Bearer <A's token>`.<br>4) Read the status and assert NOT 2xx and NOT 5xx.<br>5) JDBC: `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM <tableName("User")> WHERE id = B.id` and assert COUNT = 1.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   | • Status NOT 2xx (must be 403 or 404)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Cross-user delete succeeded — A erased B's account, irreversibly.<br>• NOT 5xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Delete pipeline threw uncaught (FK constraint, cascade error, etc.).<br>• JDBC: COUNT(*) = 1 (B still in DB)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Response was 403 but B's row was actually deleted — the delete fired before the ownership check. Particularly dangerous because subsequent test runs / production calls would all "look fine" via the response but B is gone.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| 20  | TC20 — Owner happy path: User A can UPDATE their own profile                                       | `public`, `authorization`  | `POST registerPath()` (setup), `POST loginPath()` (setup), `PUT crudReadPathFor("User", A.id)` with A's token + new name                                                                                              | The positive companion to the IDOR triad: the owner CAN update their own profile, proving the ownership check is correctly distinguishing "self" from "other" rather than blanket-rejecting all PUTs. PUT body includes all originally-registered fields plus a changed name (mitigation pattern — sloppy controllers that complain about "missing field" can't pass by accident). JDBC verification queries the database directly to confirm the update persisted, rather than depending on the GET endpoint being correct (this isolates the PUT logic from any GET-side bugs). Strict 2xx because A is acting on their own row with a valid token.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               | 1) Register A via `POST registerPath()` with a known nonce-based email and capture A's id and original phone.<br>2) Login as A and capture A's token.<br>3) Build a PUT body containing `"name":"TC20 Updated"` plus A's original email/password/phone.<br>4) PUT `crudReadPathFor("User", A.id)` with `Authorization: Bearer <A's token>` and that body.<br>5) Assert strict 2xx via `assert2xx`.<br>6) JDBC: `SELECT name FROM <tableName("User")> WHERE id = A.id` and assert it equals `"TC20 Updated"`.                                                                                                                                                                             | • PUT status 200..299 (strict)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Ownership check is too aggressive (rejects the owner's own update — false positive on IDOR check), or the PUT endpoint isn't wired at all (404).<br>• JDBC: name in DB equals `"TC20 Updated"` (the update persisted)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Controller returned 200 but the transaction rolled back or never wrote — common cause is `@Transactional` missing on the service method, or the entity is loaded in a detached state and `save` is never called. This is the positive admin-override leg of the authorization matrix: admins can read any user, including users they did not create. The test confirms the role-based bypass is wired correctly — without it, even an admin would be 403'd from reading another user's profile. Strict 2xx with JSON-object body shape, because the spec is unambiguous: ADMIN reads succeed unconditionally on existing users.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     | 1) Register a fresh attendee via PUT body includes all originally-registered fields plus a changed name (mitigation pattern — guarantees no accidental 400 from missing fields). JDBC verifies the update actually persisted to the database, isolating the PUT logic from any GET-side bugs and confirming both the response and the side-effect are correct. Strict 2xx because admin override is unconditional per spec. | 23  | TC23 — Admin override: admin can HARD-DELETE any user (happy path, strict)                         | `public`, `authorization`  | `POST registerPath()` (setup customer), `adminToken()` (setup), `DELETE crudReadPathFor("User", customer.id)` with admin token, then `GET`                                                                            | The ADMIN role must bypass ownership for delete. This test enforces a STRICT hard-delete contract: the row must be physically removed from the database (soft-delete via a status flag is explicitly NOT acceptable for this test, because the spec for User CRUD prescribes hard-delete). The GET-after-DELETE must return 404, proving the resource is genuinely unreachable post-delete. Triple verification — response status, JDBC row count, and follow-up GET — because each catches a different bug.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        | 1) Register a fresh attendee via `POST registerPath()` and capture their id.<br>2) Obtain an admin token via `adminToken()`.<br>3) DELETE `crudReadPathFor("User", attendee.id)` with `Authorization: Bearer <admin token>`.<br>4) Assert strict 2xx on the DELETE.<br>5) JDBC: `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM <tableName("User")> WHERE id = attendee.id` and assert COUNT = 0.<br>6) GET `crudReadPathFor("User", attendee.id)` with admin token and assert strictly 404.                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | • DELETE status 200..299 (strict)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Admin delete override missing.<br>• JDBC: COUNT(*) = 0 (row physically removed)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Soft-delete (status='DEACTIVATED' or active=false) instead of hard-delete — row still in DB. Strict 2xx because the owner is reading their own resource with a valid token; 401/403 here would be a policy bug, 404 means the endpoint isn't mapped, 5xx means the controller threw. With admin, the only remaining failure mode is "user not found" → 404. Strict 404 (NOT 5xx, NOT 2xx) is enforced because the spec is explicit. This catches controllers that NPE on `Optional.get()` for missing users (5xx) or that return an empty page envelope without checking the user exists (2xx with `content:[]`). doesn't validate user existence before fetching activity.<br>• NOT 5xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** `Optional.get()` on empty Optional NPEs, or repository throws on missing parent.<br>• NOT 2xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Endpoint returned an empty page envelope without checking the user actually exists — clients can't distinguish "no activity" from "no such user". NOT 5xx because a negative id is a perfectly normal HTTP path that the framework should bind successfully (negative longs are valid Long values); the controller must then validate or look up gracefully.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              | 1) Obtain an admin token via `adminToken()`.<br>2) GET `<users>/-1/activity` with `Authorization: Bearer <admin>` — `-1` binds successfully as a Java Long (it's valid).<br>3) Assert status is in 400..499 and not 5xx.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 | • Status 4xx (400 validation or 404 not-found)<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Controller doesn't reject the impossible id.<br>• NOT 5xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Repository threw on negative id (e.g., a `@Min(1)` annotation triggered a `ConstraintViolationException` that wasn't translated, or a native query NPE'd).<br>• NOT 2xx<br>&nbsp;&nbsp;- **Bug it catches:** Endpoint returned 200 with empty content, hiding the bad input.
